caveats: a: The occupation mix is for all NAICS 523000 and excludes self-employed workers.
a: Occupation employment shares are not payroll-weighted task exposure.
a: Private-fund portfolio management, retail wealth advice, and one-time financial planning have materially different task mixes.
rho: FINRA evidence is not a measured adoption rate for investment advisers.
rho: Vendor purchase and experimentation do not establish production implementation or avoided labor.
rho: Small practices may face larger integration costs while large firms face more complex governance.
e: IAA data cover SEC-registered advisers and omit many smaller state-registered firms.
e: Small employee count does not establish normalized EBITDA or transferability.
e: The supplied n is a declared dataset gap, so the eligible share cannot produce a defensible firm count without new data.
s5: Announced wealth-management deals do not equal closed qualifying control transfers.
s5: Deal definitions and coverage vary, especially for small firms and recruiting transactions.
s5: The missing frozen n prevents conversion of the probability into an expected count.
q: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefit.
q: Asset-based, performance, fixed, hourly, and subscription models pass savings through differently.
q: Market appreciation can raise revenue without reflecting operating retention.
d5: BLS output covers all NAICS 523000 rather than 523940.
d5: RIA AUM growth is heavily affected by market prices and cannot be read as service quantity.
d5: Personal-adviser employment is only one component of portfolio management and investment advice.
o: The BLS statement concerns employment, not the operator-required share of service quantity.
o: Retail planning and institutional or private-fund mandates have different substitution paths.
o: A legal adviser can remain accountable even when software performs most production work.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Individuals, families, trusts, retirement plans, institutions, and pooled vehicles pay asset-based, fixed, hourly, subscription, and performance fees for discretionary portfolio management, research, reporting, financial planning, and customized investment advice. Revenue commonly recurs with assets and client relationships, while market levels, net flows, mandates, and performance influence fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal investment offices, shells, legal funds or trusts without a separate management operation, non-control financing vehicles, publishers of generalized investment information, broker-dealers whose primary activity is transaction intermediation, and practices whose client relationships and revenue cannot transfer independently of one individual.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market portfolio managers, registered investment advisers, wealth managers, financial planners, investment counselors, trust portfolio managers, private-fund managers, and subadvisers repeatedly managing assets or providing customized advice to external clients for fees.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment counts across broad NAICS 523000 rather than payroll-weighted task exposure within 523940. I map research retrieval, meeting and call summaries, proposal and report drafting, portfolio commentary, financial-plan preparation, data reconciliation, client-service responses, onboarding, surveillance, compliance review, and administration to exposed work, then discount relationship development, individualized discovery, fiduciary judgment, negotiation, final recommendations, portfolio authority, and supervision. The broader sector's 39.70% business-and-financial, 19.18% office-support, 17.33% sales, and 15.29% management mix likely overstates transactional sales work for fee-only advisers and may understate relationship and planning work in small practices.
caveats: The occupation mix is for all NAICS 523000 and excludes self-employed workers.
Occupation employment shares are not payroll-weighted task exposure.
Private-fund portfolio management, retail wealth advice, and one-time financial planning have materially different task mixes.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-weighted time study across independent RIAs, portfolio managers, financial planners, private-fund managers, and subadvisers, separating research, portfolio decisions, planning, client meetings, service, trading, reporting, compliance, sales, and already-automated hours.
a.rationale: The industry's work is highly information-intensive and FINRA documents AI applications across customer communications, portfolio processes, compliance, risk, and administration. Many analyst, service, reporting, and documentation tasks are exposed, but advice remains customized and regulated, and clients still require accountable human judgment for complex goals and volatile markets.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output and employment for broad NAICS 523000 and employment for personal financial advisers, while IAA reports recent SEC-adviser counts, clients, employment, and AUM; none measures year-five constant-price, constant-quality demand for the combined 523940 service basket. I translate broad 2.1% annual real-output growth, adviser-industry expansion, and 9.6% ten-year personal-adviser employment growth into a five-year quantity range, discounting market-value inflation, duplicated clients, shifts between advice channels, fee compression, recessions, and private-fund cyclicality. Broader securities activity may be more cyclical than advice, while the SEC sample may overstate institutional asset growth.
caveats: BLS output covers all NAICS 523000 rather than 523940.
RIA AUM growth is heavily affected by market prices and cannot be read as service quantity.
Personal-adviser employment is only one component of portfolio management and investment advice.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit constant-price revenue and service-quantity indexes split among retail advice, institutional portfolio management, private funds, financial planning, and subadvisory mandates, with households, mandates, net flows, fee rates, client complexity, closures, and five-year forecasts.
d5.rationale: BLS projects broader NAICS 523000 real output from $721.7 billion in 2024 to $892.4 billion in 2034 and personal financial-adviser employment growth of 9.6% over the decade. IAA reports record adviser, client, employment, and asset levels in 2025. Aging, retirement complexity, wealth accumulation, private markets, and regulatory complexity support demand, but market appreciation and channel shifts are not constant-quality quantity growth.

e.bridge: Census defines 523940 as managing others' assets or providing customized advice for fees, and IAA describes SEC-registered advisers rather than the frozen LMM firm population or transferability. I treat the recurring external fee model as broadly eligible, then deduct captive or affiliated managers, legal vehicles, very small owner-practices, one-time planning businesses, entities outside the economic band, and firms whose clients or portfolio authority will not transfer. SEC-registered advisers skew larger than state-registered practices, so IAA's small-firm profile may understate founder dependence in the full industry and overstate institutionalized transferability.
caveats: IAA data cover SEC-registered advisers and omit many smaller state-registered firms.
Small employee count does not establish normalized EBITDA or transferability.
The supplied n is a declared dataset gap, so the eligible share cannot produce a defensible firm count without new data.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level match of the frozen economic-band population to Form ADV and state registrations, ownership and affiliate schedules, client type, recurring-fee mix, employee count, key-person concentration, change-of-control consent terms, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition directly matches recurring external portfolio management and customized advice. IAA reports that most SEC-registered advisers are small by employee and asset measures, but a legal advisory entity is not automatically an independently transferable control opportunity. Client consent, key-person dependence, affiliate structures, and normalized profitability reduce the usable share.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 5239 ($166,457,424,125) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 5239 ($454,212,096,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 5239. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1165 (margins.json[523]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Investments & Asset Management EBITDA/Sales 11.65%); revenue/employee $732,061 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 5239); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: Census defines operator responsibilities and BLS discusses robo-adviser substitution at the occupation level, not the year-five share of 523940 demand requiring an accountable operator after software adoption. I preserve an operator for fiduciary responsibility, client discovery, customized planning, portfolio authority, suitability, conflicts, supervision, complex tax and estate coordination, reassurance, and model oversight, while allowing standardized allocation, reporting, research, basic planning, and low-complexity advice to be self-served or delivered by software. BLS's mild employment-effect view may overstate persistence if interfaces improve rapidly and understate it where aging clients and complex needs increase human demand.
caveats: The BLS statement concerns employment, not the operator-required share of service quantity.
Retail planning and institutional or private-fund mandates have different substitution paths.
A legal adviser can remain accountable even when software performs most production work.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal client and workflow study classifying current 523940 engagements in year five as human-operated, human-supervised software, fully automated, self-directed, migrated to another provider type, or eliminated, split by wealth, age, complexity, mandate, and fee model.
o.rationale: Portfolio managers have authority over others' assets, and advisers tailor plans to client goals. BLS expects robo-advisers to have only a mild effect on personal financial-adviser employment because older clients with complex needs are unlikely to trust automated recommendations. Software can deliver standardized portfolios and information, but regulated accountability and high-stakes personalization preserve a substantial operator role.

q.bridge: Census and IAA measure fee structures, not the discounted five-year retention of AI-derived gross benefit by LMM 523940 operators. I treat recurring asset-based and fixed fees as allowing near-term retention of internal savings, then discount contract renewal, fee breakpoints, competitive fee pressure, client sharing, performance-fee allocations, market-dependent AUM, custodian and vendor charges, adviser compensation, compliance review, and reinvestment in service. The SEC-registered sample may overstate asset-based revenue relative to smaller planning firms and may understate bargaining pressure from institutional clients.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefit.
Asset-based, performance, fixed, hourly, and subscription models pass savings through differently.
Market appreciation can raise revenue without reflecting operating retention.
q.change_evidence: Adviser-level contracts and contribution-margin data showing fee type, breakpoints, billing rates, AUM and flows, labor and vendor cost by workflow, adviser payouts, client repricing, service levels, and realized margin before and after automation over five years.
q.rationale: IAA reports that 95.5% of SEC-registered advisers offered asset-based fees in 2024, while 45.2% offered fixed fees and 28.9% offered hourly fees; only 17.4% relied on asset-based fees alone. Savings inside an asset-based or fixed engagement can be retained between repricings, but clients can negotiate fees and technology can intensify price competition. Hourly work more directly passes time savings through lower billable hours.

rho.bridge: FINRA reports cautious use of vendor-supported generative AI across regulated financial firms, not the share of exposed wages implemented by LMM 523940 firms over five years. I translate current internal summarization, cross-dataset analysis, policy retrieval, customer communications, and established portfolio or compliance applications into a five-year range, then discount data integration, review, suitability and fiduciary duties, privacy, cybersecurity, recordkeeping, model governance, explainability, supervision, vendor risk, and adviser or client resistance. FINRA's broker-dealer focus may overstate some rule-specific controls but understates the fiduciary and client-trust constraints of investment advisers.
caveats: FINRA evidence is not a measured adoption rate for investment advisers.
Vendor purchase and experimentation do not establish production implementation or avoided labor.
Small practices may face larger integration costs while large firms face more complex governance.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent 523940 firms reporting deployed workflows, vendor and custodian integration, baseline and post-deployment hours, human review, errors, complaints, regulatory findings, data incidents, training cost, avoided hiring, and client retention.
rho.rationale: Financial firms are already exploring and deploying AI, but FINRA describes generative-AI adoption as cautious and centered on internal tools. Accuracy, bias, private information, supervision, and vendor controls keep human review in the workflow. Multi-site firms with standardized custodial and CRM data can implement faster than founder-led advisers with fragmented systems.

s5.bridge: ECHELON counts announced wealth-management transactions and Gallup surveys stated five-year transfer intentions across employer businesses; neither measures the five-year probability of a qualifying control transfer for an eligible LMM 523940 firm. I center on Gallup's 22% intention rate, use record wealth-management deal flow as evidence of an active channel, and discount unclosed announcements, recruiting moves, minority stakes, internal succession, subacquisitions, deals outside the economic band, and transactions without durable client-control transfer. Reported deal counts exclude very small asset firms and may undercount private deals while overrepresenting consolidator activity.
caveats: Announced wealth-management deals do not equal closed qualifying control transfers.
Deal definitions and coverage vary, especially for small firms and recruiting transactions.
The missing frozen n prevents conversion of the probability into an expected count.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census joining Form ADV and state adviser changes, SEC IARD, PitchBook, DeVoe, ECHELON, purchase agreements, and client-consent outcomes, with control stakes, closings, seller size, recurring revenue, and internal versus external succession identified.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports a 22% five-year sale or transfer intention among employer-business owners. ECHELON reports 466 announced wealth-management transactions in 2025, 27.3% more than 2024, but also says transaction tracking is imprecise and excludes deals with no more than $100 million in assets. The evidence supports an active acquisition channel without furnishing the correct firm-level denominator.

principal_driver: Recurring fee relationships and document- and data-intensive workflows create a broad opportunity to augment advisers and scale service capacity.
principal_weakness: No defensible LMM-band firm count exists, while client trust, founder dependence, fiduciary duties, and fee pressure limit both transferable supply and retained labor savings.

sources.supports: Defines fee- or commission-based management of others' portfolio assets, including decision authority and size- or performance-based fees, and customized fee-based investment advice without trade authority.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau: 2022 NAICS 523940 Portfolio Management and Investment Advice

sources.supports: Reports the broader NAICS 523000 occupation mix, including 39.70% business and financial operations, 19.18% office and administrative support, 17.33% sales, 15.29% management, and 6.45% computer and mathematical occupations.
sources.title: BLS May 2023 OEWS: Securities, Commodity Contracts, and Other Financial Investments and Related Activities

sources.supports: Documents AI use and evaluation in customer communications, brokerage-account and portfolio management, trading, compliance, risk management, and administration, along with privacy, cybersecurity, authentication, and recordkeeping risks.
sources.title: FINRA: AI Applications in the Securities Industry

sources.supports: Reports cautious financial-firm use of vendor-supported generative AI for internal summarization, cross-dataset analysis, and policy retrieval and identifies supervision, accuracy, bias, privacy, cybersecurity, data-provenance, and vendor risks.
sources.title: FINRA 2025 Annual Regulatory Oversight Report

sources.supports: Reports 2024 SEC-adviser firm-size and fee data, including median employment of eight, 95.5% offering asset-based fees, 45.2% fixed fees, 28.9% hourly fees, and only 17.4% compensated solely through asset-based fees.
sources.title: Investment Adviser Association and COMPLY: Investment Adviser Industry Snapshot 2025

sources.supports: Reports 16,544 advisers in 2025, 73.7 million clients after 7.7% growth, 1.1 million non-clerical employees after 7.5% growth, and that 92.8% of advisers employed no more than 100 people.
sources.title: Investment Adviser Association: 2026 Investment Adviser Industry Snapshot Highlights

sources.supports: Reports 466 announced wealth-management transactions in 2025, 27.3% growth from 2024, exclusions for deals with no more than $100 million in assets, and limitations in tracking smaller, internal, hybrid, and breakaway transactions.
sources.title: ECHELON Partners: 2025 RIA M&A Deal Report

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years, based on a fall 2024 survey.
sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Projects broader NAICS 523000 real output from $721.7 billion in 2024 to $892.4 billion in 2034, a 2.1% annual increase, and employment growth of 6.0% over the decade.
sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024 to 2034

sources.supports: Projects personal financial-adviser employment growth of 9.6% and states that robo-advisers are expected to have only a mild employment effect because older clients with complex planning needs are unlikely to trust automated recommendations.
sources.title: BLS Monthly Labor Review: Industry and Occupational Employment Projections, 2024 to 2034

title: Portfolio Management and Investment Advice
